source_metadata.title_raw: Caren Ray - A-18-003(a) - March 7, 2018 - Arroyo Grande

content.full_text:
, 
STATI~ OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) ;t :i.:i - 5660 • Fax (916) ~:!.:! • 0886 
Caren Ray 
Arroyo Grande City Council 
756 Myrtle Street 
Arroyo Grande, CA 93420 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-00J(a) 
Dear Ms. Ray: 
March 7, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and Government Code Section 1090. Please note that we do 
not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of 
interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 
71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Luis Obispo 
County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097. l(c)(3).) We did not receive 
a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you 
that, for purposes of Section I 090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097. l(c)(5).) 
· 
QUESTION 
Does the Act or Section 1090 prohibit you, a city council member who owns property 
currently housing an Airbnb rental subject to a Transient Occupancy Tax ("TOT'), from talcing part 
in decisions regarding a contract with Airbnb to collect these taxes on behalf of the City of Arroyo 
Grande? 
CONCLUSION 
Neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibit you from taking part in decisions to contract with 
Airbnb for the collection of TOT taxes, so long as it applies equally to all other properties within 
the City subject to the tax. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code-of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-003(a) 
Page No. 2 
The City currently collects a TOT and other truces that go toward tourism-generating entities 
from all hotels, vacation rentals, and short-term rentals such as Airbnb. This amounts to a 13% true 
on room rentals throughout the City. You are currently an Airbnb host. 
You state that the collection of these truces has been problematic for the City. Collection 
largely relies on the honor system. It has come to the City's attention that there are over 50 short-
term Airbnb rentals operating in the City, yet there are only six that are legally permitted and 
paying the associated truces. You note that your property is one of the six. 
Like many small cities, Arroyo Grande needs help to enforce collection of a TOT and 
related truces. It is extremely difficult to identify who is operating legally and illegally on the Airbnb 
site. To this end, the County of San Luis Obispo has contracted directly with Airbnb to collect a 
TOT and related truces on its behalf. This effectively would bring all Airbnb rentals within their 
jurisdiction into compliance from a true-collection point of view. In your original request for advice, 
you stated that the "[C]ity is contemplating entering into a contract with the County of San Luis 
Obispo to collect these truces." On February 21, 2018, you stated that this was not the case, and that 
the "contract would be directly with Airbnb, not with the County. "2 
It is unknown at this time if Airbnb would charge a service fee to its hosts to collect the true 
during the booking process. You note that the realistic financial effects on you could range from no 
effect at all (because you are already in compliance) to increased fees assessed directly to you from 
Airbnb. 
ANALYSIS 
The Political Reform Act 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own interests or the interests of persons who 
have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, 
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to _influence a governmental 
decision in which the official has an interest. The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to 
conflicts of interest arising from enumerated interests in Section 87103. The facts you have 
provided indicate the following interests are implicated by the decision: 
• 
An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 8 7103( d) ). 
• 
An interest in real property (presumably worth $2,000 or more). (Section 87103(b).) 
2 Ray Advice Letter No. A-18-003 is SUPERSEDED by Ray Advice Letter No. A-18-003(a) 


Foreseeability a11d Materiality 
Foreseeability 
File No. A-18-003(a) 
Page No. 3 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701.) Where the interest is explicitly involved, a financial 
effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. None of your interests are explicitly involved in the 
decision by the City to contract with the County for the collection of TOT taxes. 
Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, such as here, the 
foreseeability standard is whether the financial effect can be recognized as a "realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Materiality 
Business Interests: Regulation 18702.1 (b) sets forth the applicaqle materiality standard for a 
business interest. 3 Regulation 18702.1 (b) states in pertinent part as follows: 
"For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the financial effect 
is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a 
change in ... the value of a privately-held business entity." 
Nothing in your facts suggest that the decision to contract with Airbnb for the collection of 
TOT taxes, which you are already paying, will contribute to a change in the value of your privately-
held business interest as an Airbnb host. 
Real Property Interest: A financial effect on real property is material if it meets any of the 
standards in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) through (12). The standard relevant to the decision in 
question is whether the decision: 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property." 
There no facts suggesting that the decision wilJ affect the value of your property. 
Consequently, based on your facts, you would not have a conflict of interest in the decision under 
the Act. 
3 Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's 
interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that if the source of income is a business entity, 
materiality is determined pursuant to the standard for materiality for the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a 
business entity contained in Regulation 18702.1. 


File No. A-18-003(a) 
· 
Page No. 4 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, County, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Section I 090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member bodies, 
whether elected or appointed and covers a broad range of actions by a public official other than 
actually approving a contract, including actions involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, 
compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. 
In this instance, you are a public officer subject to the provisions of Section 1090 
Additionally, as a member of the Arroyo Grande City Council, you are presumed to be involved in 
the making of all contracts by the City irrespective of whether you actually participate in the 
making of the contract. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 645, 649.) Thus, the determinative question is 
whether you have a financial interest in a contract if the City contracts with Airbnb to collect TOT 
truces. 
Generally, a "financial interest" is broadly defined for purposes of Section 1090 and 
includes any monetary or proprietary benefit, or gain of any sort, or the contingent possibility of 
monetary or proprietary benefits. Moreover, the term financial interest has been applied by the 
courts in a variety of contexts. (See Bordsen Advice Letter, No. A-17-059.) However, under the 
facts provided, the TOT already applies to your property and you are currently paying all truces due. 
The contract with Airbnb would merely facilitate the City's ability to collect the TOT from 
delinquent property owners, the City cannot easily identify. Under these circumstances, we do not 
find that an official has a financial interest in a contract to collect truces merely because the official's 
business, as well as all similarly operated businesses, are subject to the true. Accordingly, we 
conclude that Section 1090 does not apply under the facts presented. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
